problem_summary: 2014 MCM/ICM Problem B (discrete, "best all-time college coach"): Sports Illustrated asks for a model to identify the best all-time college coach (male or female) of the previous century, to test whether a time-line horizon (e.g., 1913 vs. 2013) matters, and to present results for 3 different sports plus a non-technical write-up. Team 26160 framed it as a multi-criteria ranking/evaluation problem: distill ~54 candidate criteria down to 7 quantifiable ones via AHP, score and rank ~100 real coaches (drawn from NCAA/Wikipedia win-leader lists) per sport with two independent methods, validate against published "current" rankings, then perturb weights to add a time factor and run sensitivity analysis. Demonstrated on basketball (46 coaches), college field hockey, and football.
models_used: AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) — 3-level hierarchy (Comprehensive impact -> personal/training/team-achievement -> 9 leaf criteria) with Saaty 1-9 pairwise comparison matrices, eigenvalue weights, and consistency ratio (CR) checks (CR=0.0043, 0.0001, 0.0037 etc.) to derive criterion weights and filter to 7 criteria

Grey Relational Analysis (Grey Correlation Model) — min-max normalization (separate positive/negative formulas), ideal reference series A0=(1,...,1), grey relational coefficient with resolution ratio beta=0.5, criterion correlation degrees re-normalized into weights, then linear aggregate score Z_i (their primary/winning model)

Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation (multi-level) — 5-star comment set {5,4,3,2,1}, linear membership functions to build membership matrix D, AHP weights as B vector, fuzzy composition D = B o R using operators M(dot, tensor) with a*b=ab and a tensor b=(a+b) wedge 1, final score Z=B·D^T

Weighted-overlay time-horizon extension — reclaimed the ~7% weight dropped during criterion filtering, assigned it to a normalized start-year time score Z2=(T-1900)/100, recombined as Z = 0.93·Z1 + 0.07·Z2 to test time-line influence
Sensitivity analysis — swept grey resolution ratio beta over [0,1] (step 0.2) to find a stable regime (beta>0.3), and perturbed the max fuzzy weight by +/-0.02 to confirm rank stability
MATLAB used as the computational engine for the grey and fuzzy scoring
key_techniques: Two-stage pipeline: AHP as a dimensionality-reduction/weighting front-end feeding two independent back-end scoring models, rather than a single monolithic model

Cross-validation by model triangulation: running grey vs. fuzzy and comparing both against an external ground-truth (Wikipedia/NCAA 'current rank'), then explicitly declaring grey the better model because it tracked the reference rank more closely

Quantifying soft/qualitative factors: gender encoded as a numeric handicap (male=1, female=3) with a stated justification; 'performance in league matches' operationalized per-sport (NCAA Final Four appearances for basketball, top-4 finishes for football/field hockey)
Consistency-ratio gating on every AHP matrix to demonstrate the subjective pairwise judgments were internally coherent
Transferability claim made concrete: the same 7-criterion model re-applied unchanged across basketball, field hockey, and football to show sport-independence
Treating the dropped ~7% AHP weight as a 'budget' later spent on the time criterion — a tidy way to bolt the time-horizon question onto the existing model without re-deriving weights
Full reproducibility scaffolding: raw per-coach data tables for all three sports and both-model score/rank tables included in the appendix
why_it_won: From a judge's view, this paper wins on completeness, validation discipline, and presentation rather than on mathematical novelty. It (1) directly and visibly answers all three problem sub-tasks including the often-skipped time-line question and the required Sports Illustrated letter; (2) uses two independent, well-known evaluation methods and — crucially — does not just report both but adjudicates between them against an external ground truth, which reads as intellectual honesty and gives the ranking credibility; (3) explicitly handles the problem's two 'gotcha' requirements (gender, and applicability to many sports) with concrete encodings and a genuine cross-sport demonstration; (4) grounds everything in real, sourced NCAA/Wikipedia data with a CR-checked weighting scheme so the subjective AHP judgments look principled; and (5) is heavily and clearly visualized (hierarchy diagrams, ranked bar charts, three-way rank-comparison line chart, sensitivity plots) with a clean front-summary that states the top-5 tables up front. The sensitivity analysis on both the grey resolution ratio and the fuzzy weights closes the loop on robustness. Judges reward this 'answer-everything, validate-it, show-it clearly' package.
section_highlights: Summary (lines 7-23): states methods, hands over the final top-5 tables immediately, and pre-announces the key finding that grey slightly beats fuzzy — a model of front-loading conclusions. Model Overview + flowchart (Figure 1, lines 104-141): one diagram showing the whole AHP -> {grey, fuzzy} -> rank -> Wikipedia-comparison pipeline. AHP section (Tables 1-6, lines 205-247): four pairwise matrices each with lambda_max and CR reported, then a combination-weight vector used to prune from 9 to 7 criteria — the analytical backbone. Grey model steps 1-7 (lines 340-386): clean, numbered, reproducible algorithm with the reference series and resolution ratio spelled out. Results Review (Section 6.4, Table 15 + Figure 9, lines 689-726): the decisive three-way grey/fuzzy/current rank comparison that justifies choosing grey. Time-line section (Section 7, lines 728-841): reuses the leftover 7% weight, then verifies the 'time hits lower-ranked coaches more' claim on a second sport (football). Sensitivity (Section 8) and Strengths/Weaknesses (Section 9.3, lines 973-986): honest self-critique (ignored SRS/SOS, thin female-coach data, no subjective factors). The Honor Roll and Sports Illustrated letter (lines 933-1014) deliver the required non-technical communication with personality.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Survivorship/selection bias baked into the data: coaches were drawn only from 'most wins' leader lists (600+ basketball wins, 200+ football wins, 250+ field-hockey wins), so the pool is pre-filtered to high win-totals — the 'best' is found inside an already-elite, longevity-skewed sample, which inflates win/longevity criteria.

The gender encoding (female=3 vs male=1) is a crude, unvalidated thumb-on-the-scale; the justifications offered are sociological assertions, and it is also confounded with the fact that the female-coach pools are sport-segregated (field hockey is essentially all female).

Circular validation: the model is judged 'good' by how closely it matches the Wikipedia/'current' rank, yet that same ranking shaped criterion choice — agreement with the reference is partly engineered, not independent confirmation.

Inconsistent and partly broken figures: the bar chart captioned 'field hockey' shows basketball names, several charts are mislabeled, and the fuzzy field-hockey/football bar charts show absurd y-axis values (~5000) — sloppiness that survived into the final paper.

Self-contradictory text in the results discussion (line 722 says 'Fuzzy ... considers inner link ... while the Fuzzy ... doesn't', clearly meant grey vs fuzzy) — an editing error in the load-bearing comparison sentence.
The 'race command capability' (RCC) criterion appears to be a derived/opaque integer with no transparent definition or source formula, weakening reproducibility despite the appendix data dump.

Time-horizon treatment is shallow: a fixed 7%/93% linear blend with Z2=(T-1900)/100 is an arbitrary proxy; it conflates 'start year' with 'era difficulty' and never models how criteria importance actually shifts over a century.

Acknowledged but unaddressed: ignores Strength of Schedule (SOS), Simple Rating System (SRS), consecutive wins, and all qualitative/ethical factors — so the ranking rewards padding records against weak schedules.
reusable_patterns: AHP-as-front-end pattern: use AHP with Saaty 1-9 matrices + CR checks to both weight and prune a large candidate-criteria set down to a tractable, defensible few — reusable for any MCM evaluation/ranking problem.

Dual-model triangulation + external-ground-truth adjudication: solve the same ranking with two independent methods (e.g., grey relational vs. fuzzy comprehensive), compare against a real-world reference list, and explicitly pick the better one — turns 'we tried two things' into a credibility argument.

Quantifying qualitative requirements: explicitly encode a soft factor the prompt demands (here gender) as a numeric value with a written rationale, and operationalize vague metrics per-context (per-sport 'league performance') — a template for handling fuzzy problem requirements.

Weight-budget reuse for add-on questions: when AHP filtering leaves residual weight, spend that exact leftover (7%) on the secondary question's new factor (time) via a simple convex blend Z=rho1·Z1+rho2·Z2 — a low-risk way to extend a finished model.

Robustness twofer: do sensitivity analysis on a model-internal hyperparameter (grey resolution ratio beta sweep to find a stable regime) AND on the input weights (+/-0.02 perturbation) to argue stability from two angles.

Deliverables-first writing: lead the summary with the final answer tables, give a single end-to-end pipeline flowchart, and include all raw data + both-model score tables in the appendix for reproducibility.

Grey Relational Analysis recipe for normalized multi-criteria scoring: ideal reference series of all-1s, positive/negative min-max normalization, relational coefficient with beta=0.5, correlation-degree-derived weights, linear aggregate — a portable scoring kernel.
